item.title: Mark Kermode reviews Blue Road: The Edna O’Brien Story - Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
A 93-year-old Irish writer Edna O'Brien recounts her controversial life, novels, love affairs, and stardom through personal journals read by actress Jessie Buckley, with perspectives from writers like Gabriel Byrne and Walter Mosley.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews The Penguin Lessons - Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
A disillusioned Englishman who goes to work in a school in a divided Argentina in 1976 finds his life transformed when he rescues an orphaned penguin from the beach.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: Star Trek: The Motion Picture - re:View (Part 2)
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded: Rich has always been salty at Mike for not included him in his original review of Star Trek The Motion picture with Canadian Clown™ Jimbo. Rich loves this movie so much he can barely remember anything about it and needs to be constantly reminded which scenes are which. However, after the release of the beautiful 4K restoration, Mike felt like revisiting this dusty old classic. He does so with minimal effort, examining all the changes to this film, while getting numerous things wrong and very little correct. It's time for the home! Rushed into theaters for no real reason in 1979. The film was not really done, but Shatner had rented a new toupee for the premiere and he needed to return it, so he insisted they rush out the print. Then in 1983 they made a longer 3 hour TV version for virgins to watch on the other network when the Super Bowl™ was on. Then in 2001 there was a directors cut supervised by Robert Wise himself! Now the ultimate 4K directors cut has been released (which I think includes the other versions) so much has changed and been removed and unfinished it's hard to keep track of. There is also the "Memory Wall" sequence which we didn't get into cause it's REALLY unfinished and I just don't care one bit. All joking aside, Rich and Mark really do love this movie. It's slow, it's dull, and it's mature and lacks punching and a villain with a super-weapon. It's core Star Trek when Star Trek was really for nerds and not jocks that like explosions and punching.

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews One to One: John & Yoko - Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Set in 1972 New York, this documentary explores John and Yoko's world amid a turbulent era. Centered on the One to One charity concert for special needs children, it features unseen archives, home movies, and restored footage.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://listen.sonymusic-podcasts.link/TFTF28194?sid=kermodeandmayo.com

item.title: Probably not caught today🐟🤮 #movie #horrorfilm #comedy
item.author: FanboyFlicks

item.title: When an Actor Stopped Holding Back - Al Pacino
item.author: FilmStack
item.content:encoded:
Al Pacino wasn’t just one of the greatest actors of his generation, he was two of them. In the 70s, Pacino became one of the faces of New Hollywood. His performances in The Godfather, Serpico, and Dog Day Afternoon weren’t flashy or theatrical, they were raw, restrained, and deeply human. His intensity was internal, which made the moments when he did erupt feel that much more unforgettable. But by the 90s, something had changed. Pacino was commanding every frame. Scent of a Woman, Heat, The Devil’s Advocate, his performances had become bolder, louder, and more theatrical.
The shift wasn’t random. A lifetime in theatre had always pulled him towards that style. He damaged his voice, which only made him sound more intense. And after a brutal decade in the 80s, full of flops that almost ended his career, he came back and embraced everything he used to hold back on. This is the story of when an actor stopped holding back. This is Al Pacino.
Chapters: 00:00 - Intro 00:56 - Rise & Reserved (70s) 04:38 - Peak 70s Run 09:03 - Almost Quit (80s) 12:57 - Louder Than Ever (90s) 19:38 - All Over The Place (2000s+)
Al Pacino. Greatest Actor. Oscar Best Actor. Loudest Actor. The Godfather. Michael Corleone. Serpico. The Godfather Part 2. Dog Day Afternoon. The Irishman. Robert De Niro. And Justice For All. Scarface. Cruising. Tony Montana. Sea of Love. The Godfather Part 3. Glengarry Glen Ross. Scent of a Woman. Heat. Donnie Brasco. Carlito's Way. The Insider. The Devil's Advocate. Insomnia. Christopher Nolan. Jack and Jill. Best Theatre Actors.
If you like this video don't forget to leave a like, and if you're interested in videos about movies and the film industry in general, make sure to subscribe to FilmStack for more great content.
If you have any other ideas for videos, leave a comment and I might make a video with your idea.
Patreon: https://patreon.com/FilmStack Twitter: https://twitter.com/realFilmStack Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/realfilmstack/
#alpacino #dedication #bestactor

item.title: Half in the Bag: A Minecraft Movie
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded: We saw The Minecraft Movie! Correction: we saw A Minecraft Movie. This movie is a Minecraft movie, because someone had to make it. It stars Jack Black because of course it does. In the film, lights and colors appear on the screen and people in the audience laugh when characters say that their butt hurts. It is truly a magical time at the cinema and we can only hope that this Minecraft Movie is not the only Minecraft Movie.

item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:

item.title: Best of the Worst: Terror Squad, Back from Hell, and Traxx
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded: JRT returns to RLM for another BOTW. On this jam-packed episode, the gang watches an action movie sandwich with an embarrassing amateur Evil Dead knock off in the middle. First up is Terror Squad starring Chuck Conners. Who is Chuck Conners, you say? Yes! Terror Squad was shot in beautiful Kokomo, Indiana! Next up is Back From Hell, a no-budget 16mm extravaganza about 20 year olds running around the words pretending to be demons or something. Back from Hell was shot in beautiful Ann Arbor, Michigan! And finally it's Traxx!!!! The starring vehicle for veteran voice actor Shadoe Stevens. A confusing hybrid of action, comedy, parody, violent murder, and farts, Traxx left the gang stunned. Traxx was shot in beautiful backlot!

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews A Working Man - Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Levon Cade left his profession behind to work construction and be a good dad to his daughter. But when a local girl vanishes, he's asked to return to the skills that made him a mythic figure in the shadowy world of counter-terrorism.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297
